texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solubility  of  NaO  in pure  alkanolamines (MEA,  DEA,  DIPA,  TEA,  MDEA  and AMP) has been  measured over  the  temperature range  from  20  to  85  'C  at  atmospheric pressure  and  correlated as  an  exponential function  of  temperature. The  densities  of  the  pure  alkanolamlnes  were  determlned at  temperatures from 20  to  90  'C. A  semiempirical model  for  CalcuIating  the  solubility of  NaO  in  amine  mixtures  is  presented. The model is  based on  the  work by  Prausnitz and  Chueh,  by  Prausnitz and  by  Boublik and  Hala. Correlations of  experimental NzO  solubility  data are  carried  out for  eight  systems  (MDEA-HzO, AMP-HzO, DIPA-HzO, DEA-HzO, MEA-HaO,  AMP-MDEA-HzO, AMP-TMS-Hz0 and DEA-EG-HzO) between  15 and 70  'C  over  a  large  range  of  concentrations. Comparison with  the  experimental results  indicates  that  the model should  be  reliable for estimating the  solubility of  NzO  in  alkanolamine mixtures  with  binary  or ternary solvents. The solubility data  for pure  amines  and  the  correlation for  mixed solvents may  be applied to  estimate  the  solubility of  COz  gas  in  pure  or  mixed  solvents  assuming  the  NaO-CO2 analogy.

texts.orig: Alkanolamine solutions are  widely used  for  the removal of  acid  gases  from  natural  gases  and  synthesis  gas  streams.  Conventionally, aqueous  monoethanolamine (MEA), diethanolamine (DEA), di-isopropanolamine (DIPA), and methyldiethanolamine (MDEA)  solutions  are examples  of well-known and  industrially important amine  solutions [ 1,  21. Recently, sterically hindered amines such as 2amino-2-methyl-l -propanol (AMP) have  been  proposed as  commercially attractive new  solvents for the  gas  purification process [3,  41. Aqueous a&anolamine solutions are  more widely used  than  hot potassium carbonate solutions since they  require less  energy and  have  lower corrosion rates.  Also, blending of  ammes  is  considered to  be  attractive and  blends are  more flexible than  single amines because the  high capacity of  one  amine  may  be combined with high absorption rates of another amine  and  the  relative concentration of  the  total amine  can  be  varied.

Good solubility data  for gases  in aqueous  amine  solutions  are  not  plentiful, while solubility  data in ternary  solvents  are especially scarce.  Although some  solubility data  for NaO  in various  amine  solutions  have  been  published  in the open  literature [5-l 51, these  data are  scattered  and solubilities of NaO  in  pure amine solvents have rarely  been  measured.

texts.orig: For estimating the required solubility data at certain  temperatures and  concentrations of  amine mixtures from available experimental data, researchers  have looked  for  good  methods  to correlate them  so  as to  reduce  the  experimental effort.  There have  been  several  treatments  for  gas  solubilities in mixed  solvents.  These  approaches  have used specific expansion formulae for the  Gibbs  free energy in the  multicomponent mixture,  such  as the  one-term Margules equation [ 16 1, the  Kohler  equation [ 171, the  Wilson equation, the  Wohl expansion and  the van  Laar  model [ 181.  The equations obtained by these  methods  involve  some  coefficients  which  correspond  to  interaction  between the  various  solvent species. texts.orig: In this research  the  solubility  data  of  N,O  in each of  the  pure  amine  solvents  MEA,  DEA,  DIPA,  TEA, MDEA  and AMP  were  presented over  the  range  of temperatures from 20  to  85  'C.  The densities of the  amines  which  were  involved in  this  study  were determined as well. With the aid of molecular-thermodynamic work developed by Prausnitz and  Chueh [ 191, by  Prausnitz [20] and  by  Boublik and H6la [ 211, an effective  mathematical  model  was investigated to  evaluate the  solubilities of  NaO  in mixed amine solvents over a  wide range of  concentrations  and temperatures. By  means  of  the N,O analogy [ 15,  22,  231 the  solubility data  for  CO,  in these systems can  be estimated from the corresponding data  for  NzO.

texts.orig: Aqueous alkanolamine solutions  present  a strong polar  character  which  makes  an alkanolamine easily form a  hydrogen bond  with  water. Therefore the weaker  van  der  Waals  forces are  less  important in the solution and  the  mixed solvents. The simple method of  the  sum  of  the  product of  the  volume or  mole  fraction and  Henry's coefficient cannot  be used to  estimate  the solubility  in the mixed  solution. The  excess quantity over the  sum  of  the  product of  the  volume or  mole fraction and  Henry's coef- ficient  is traditionally considered as an excess  thermodynamic quantity  of  the  mixed  solvent.  Nitta  et al.  [24]  discovered that the  excess  quantitites  over the  sum  of  the  volume fraction contributions are closer to zero than  those over the  sum  of  mole fraction contributions in their  work.  For  the  mixed solvent  system, the sum of the product  of the volume fraction  and individual  Henry's  coefficients  has been used  to  describe the  'excess Henry's coefficient' as  follows:

texts.orig: Here  qj is the two-body interaction  parameter,  and A contains three-body  and higher-order  terms. These interaction  parameters,  such as two-body  and threebody parameters and  so on,  all  depend on  the temperature and  the  nature of the  components. Therefore there  is  no  general  equation  which  contains  these  fixed two-body interaction parameters to  describe all  mixed alkanolamine solutions. For certain systems,  the  parameters, which  are  based on experimental  data, may be expressed  as a function of temperature  and the concentrations  of the solvents [21,  241. In this  study,  eqn.  (5) is terminated  after two-body terms  in binary  solvent  systems  or  threebody terms in  ternary solvent systems  since  this simplification can  provide a  sufficiently good approximation for  these  mixed  alkanolamine solvent systems.

texts.orig: Solubilities  for  pure  amines  were  measured  in  a glass  flask  with  a  volume of 250  ml  at  the  local pressure  of  about 94  kPa.  The  experimental  apparatus and operating  method  used were  similar to that of  Haimour  and Sandall [ 111. The  principle  of this method was to bring a known volume  of solution into contact  with a gas in a closed  system at constant temperature and pressure. Equilibrium was reached ,by stirring the solution until no change  in the volume of the gas was observed.  The volume of gas absorbed was obtained.  The absorption  apparatus, which was connected to  the  volumetric  apparatus with a very narrow tube, was kept at constant temperature inside a water bath whose temperature could be maintained constant within  + 0.05  K. Meanwhile, the apparatus for the volumetric  measurement was kept at ambient temperature  which  was  also  kept  constant  within + 0.2  K. The system was first purged  with saturated NzO  gas.  Then,  the  inlet  and  outlet  valves  were closed. Ensuring  that  the  pressure  in  the  flask  is atmospheric, the  initial  position of water  in  the burette  can  be  determined.  About  40  ml  of  amine solvent  was weighed  by  a balance  whose  precision is f  1 x low6  kg,  and  then  was  injected  into  the flask.  By  means  of  the  density  measured  in  this work,  the  volume of  solution  is  obtained.  Mixing NaO with  solution  is  performed using  a  magnetic stirring  bar. The position of water in the two branches is levelled  every  few  minutes to  ensure that the gas phase  is kept  at atmospheric  pressure.  When  equilibrium  is  reached,  the  change  in  gas  volume  can be measured  using the cathetometer.  The measured change  in  gas  volume is  equal  to  the  volume of solution  sample minus the volume  of  gas absorbed. The  partial pressure  of  NaO in the  experiment  was corrected for  the  vapour  pressure  of  the  solvent. The  maximum  experimental  error  in the measured gas  solubiliiy  was  estimated  to  be + 2%.  This apparatus and method  have previously  been  used and calibrated  by  Xu et al. [ 131.

texts.orig: Figure  1 shows that the densities  of various  amines increase with  increase in  the  molecular weight of amine,  such  as  MEA, DEA  and  TEA. However, if the amines  are  isomers, they do not follow the above rule.  For  example, although the  molecular weight of  AMP or  DIPA  is  larger  than  that  of  MEA or  DEL4 respectively, their  densities are  less  than those  of  MEA and  DEA. To some  extent, density is  associated with  the  molecular structure  and  the distance  between molecules. Also, the  absorption capability of  acid  gases  in  amines  may  be  affected by an  isomeric or sterically hindered structure. Figure 1 shows  that the  experimental values  are  in good agreement with  some  available density  data for MEA and  DEA and  for MDEA, which were published  by  Murrieta-Guevara and  Rodriguez [28] and  Al-Ghawas et al. [9] respectively.

texts.orig: From  Table  6, it is readily  observed that, for  the MDEA-Ha0 system in  which more than  100  experimental data  were offered, although  some  data differ  by  as  much  as  250  kPa  m3 kmol-  ' between different  workers  who applied  different  experimental techniques, the  correlation equation covers all  the temperatures and  concentrations with  an  average deviation of  the  correlation of  less  than  4.3%. Versteeg  and van  Swaaij  [8]  previously correlated their solubility data  with  polynomial functions at  each temperature. There  were six  empirical correlation equations for six temperatures. Some equations required more  than  five  coefficients. Some  of  the solubility data  from  Al-Ghawas  et al.  [9] were  also correlated by  an empirical equation  using  the leastsquares technique at  the specified experimental conditions. The correlation equation required 12 coefficients  including  the factors  of temperature  and MDEA  mass fraction,  since the model  was not based on  a  thermodynamic model. With eqns.  (13) and (14), all available  solubility  data for  the MDEA-HZ0 system  were  correlated only  with  four  parameters. Compared  with  correlation results  by  Versteeg and van Swaaij  [S] for the DIPA-Ha0 system, it is obvious that  the  model still  has  advantages due  to  fewer coefficients and  the  need  for only  one  correlation equation.
